source: All-In Podcast — E221: AI cold war, Signalgate, CoreWeave IPO, tariffs
summary: CoreWeave may be significantly underappreciated — running large GPU clusters at scale is genuinely hard and not a commodity
summary_extended: Baker pushes back on the prevailing negative consensus around CoreWeave's IPO, arguing that the company is meaningfully underappreciated because operating large-scale GPU clusters is far more operationally complex than the market appreciates. He contends that managing tens of thousands of synchronized GPUs — dealing with thermal failures, cable issues, and training data loss — is genuinely difficult, and that CoreWeave executes this as well as any operator in the industry. Baker uses the analogy of running 1,000 well-operated retail stores: it sounds simple in theory, but very few organizations can actually do it at scale. He also points to CoreWeave's acquisition of Weights & Biases as a differentiating strategic move that goes beyond pure infrastructure commodity provision. The investment implication is that CRWV may be mispriced at IPO due to an overly dismissive market narrative, though Baker frames this as a moderate conviction view rather than a strong buy recommendation.
detail: Baker pushes back on consensus negative sentiment on CoreWeave's IPO. He argues that operating tens of thousands of synchronized GPUs reliably (managing thermal failures, cable issues, training data loss) is operationally complex and that CoreWeave does it as well as anyone. He compares it to running 1,000 well-operated retail stores — sounds easy, very few can do it. He also notes the Weights & Biases acquisition as a differentiating move.
confidence_evidence: Baker says 'I think it's a good company' and 'I do think CoreWeave runs these big GPU clusters as well as anyone' but frames it as an offsetting positive to consider rather than a strong buy recommendation.
quote_fragment: it may not be the commodity that everyone thinks it is

ticker_audit.reasoning: CoreWeave is the explicit and sole subject of this thesis — Baker is making a specific contrarian case for the company's operational differentiation and potential undervaluation at IPO.
